context: The time, the period of the judges, every man was doing that which was right in his own eyes.
point_summary: In contrast to the era of the judges—when every man did what was right in his own eyes—God's statutes are always right, offering an objective standard that human perspective, being flawed, cannot supply.

context: I think about the request that is made in Judges chapter number 13, and this is a request that is made, and a request that every parent ought to make.
point_summary: In Judges 13:8, Manoah's prayer that God would send the man of God again to teach them how to raise the promised child is held up as a model request every parent ought to make.

context: then Manoah entreated the Lord and said, O my Lord, let the man of God, which thou didst send, come again unto us, notice this, and teach us what we shall do unto the child that shall be born.
point_summary: Manoah's prayer that God would send instruction on how to raise the promised child illustrates the posture every parent should take—acknowledging dependence on divine teaching for the task of parenting.

context: neither did Manasseh drive out the inhabitants of Bethshean and her towns, nor Panach and her towns, nor the inhabitants of Dor and her towns, nor the inhabitants of Iblim and her towns, nor the inhabitants of Megiddo and her towns, but the Canaanites would dwell in that land.
point_summary: Judges 1:27–35 is cited as corroborating evidence that Noah's curse on Canaan—"a servant of servants"—was historically fulfilled as Canaanite peoples became tributaries under Israelite dominion rather than being driven out.

context: and it came to pass when Israel was strong that they put the Canaanites to tribute, but did not, and did not, excuse me, utterly drive them out.
point_summary: Judges 1:28, alongside parallel passages in Joshua 16–17, is cited as fulfillment of Noah's curse that Canaan would be "a servant of servants," with the Canaanites reduced to tributary status under Israel.

context: Heres and Ajalon, and then shelled them, and the hand of the house of Joseph prevailed, so they became tributaries. And the coast of the Amorites was from the going up to Acribim, from the rock and upward. You notice several times within the passage in Judges chapter number 1, the Bible mentions the Canaanites dwelling there and being under tribute, being servants.
point_summary: Judges 1's repeated refrain that Israel's tribes failed to drive out the Canaanites but instead put them to tribute is presented as the historical fulfillment of Noah's curse that Canaan would be a servant.

context: look over the book of judges if you will in chapter number six and judges chapter number six and verse number 24
point_summary: Jehovah Shalom, meaning "the Lord is peace," is revealed in Judges 6:24 through Gideon's experience, demonstrating that God's names were given to increase the faith of His people.

context: when Gideon perceived that he was an angel of the Lord Gideon said alas O Lord God for because I have seen an angel of the face of the Lord
point_summary: Gideon's fearful encounter with the angel of the Lord, and God's reassuring word "peace be unto thee," becomes the occasion for naming the altar Jehovah Shalom—the Lord is peace.

context: the Lord said unto him peace be unto thee fear not thou shalt not die
point_summary: Jehovah Shalom — the LORD is peace — is revealed in God's direct word to the fearful Gideon, "peace be unto thee, fear not," prompting Gideon to build an altar of worship in response.

context: then Gideon built an altar there unto the Lord and called it Jehovah Shalom
point_summary: Gideon's act of building an altar and naming it Jehovah Shalom is presented as the fitting worshipful response to God's direct assurance of peace, revealing that the Lord himself is the source and substance of that peace.

context: God one of his names has been given as Jehovah Shalom
point_summary: Jehovah Shalom reveals that God's peace is not the absence of trouble but his own presence with his people in the midst of it.

context: every man did that which was right in his own eyes
point_summary: Judges 21:25 grounds the warning that self-defined righteousness—every man doing what is right in his own eyes—stands in direct contrast to God's revealed standard of justice in Micah 6:8.

context: And we find that you could look at that at Judges 13 and verse number two.
point_summary: Samson, the most prominent judge in Israel's history, was of the tribe of Dan, fulfilling Jacob's prophecy that Dan would judge his people.

context: the Bible says, and the children of Dan set up the graven image.
point_summary: Dan's introduction of idolatry into Israel is grounded in Judges 18:30, where the tribe formally established graven image worship with a priestly line that persisted until the captivity.

context: And Jonathan, the son of Gershom, the son of Manasseh, he and his sons were priests to the tribe of Dan until the day of the captivity of the land.
point_summary: Judges 18:30 records the tribe of Dan establishing a graven image and a priestly line to serve it, fulfilling Jacob's prophecy by identifying Dan as the tribe that introduced idolatry to Israel.

context: And they set them up Micah's graven image, which he made all the time that the house of God was in Shiloh.
point_summary: The tribe of Dan's establishment of Micah's graven image at Shiloh illustrates how Dan introduced idolatry into Israel, fulfilling Jacob's prophecy of Dan as a serpent causing the nation to stumble.

context: as we read in Judges 18, where it's Dan that introduced that idol worship
point_summary: Dan's prior embrace of idolatry in Judges 18 is cited as the reason Jeroboam strategically placed one of his golden calves there—the tribe was already conditioned to accept idol worship.

context: Seven times in the book of Judges, we read this statement that the children of Israel did evil, seven times.
point_summary: Seven times in Judges the refrain "the children of Israel did evil" marks a recurring cycle in which moral autonomy—every man doing what was right in his own eyes—invites divine judgment.

context: really a theme of the book of Judges is that every man did that which was right in his own sight.
point_summary: Seven times in Judges the refrain "the children of Israel did evil" marks a recurring cycle in which moral autonomy—every man doing what was right in his own eyes—invites divine judgment and then gracious deliverance.

context: Can remind you this morning of two Old Testament passages of scripture. Joshua chapter number seven and Judges chapter number seven
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Judges chapter 7, the life of Gideon, we're familiar with again,
point_summary: Self-dependence leads to defeat, as illustrated by Gideon's story where God deliberately reduced Israel's army so no one could claim the victory as their own.

context: and the Lord said unto Gideon, the people that are with thee are too many for me to give the Midianites into their hands.
point_summary: God deliberately reduced Gideon's army so Israel could not credit the victory to themselves, illustrating the danger of self-dependence after triumph.

context: the Lord said unto Gideon, by the 300 men that lapped will I save you and deliver the Midianites into thine hand
point_summary: God deliberately reduced Gideon's army to 300 so that the resulting victory over Midian could only be attributed to God, not human strength.

context: I'm reminded specifically of Gideon. Remember when God first comes to Gideon and thou mighty man of valor
point_summary: God's address to Gideon as a "mighty man of valor" illustrates how God builds faith incrementally in those who doubt their own calling.

context: of Judges, I mean the Old Testament, Judges chapter number 13, this morning, Judges chapter number 13, and with each passing year, I realize personally what a great honor and responsibility it is to have children.
point_summary: Parenting at every stage carries both honor and weighty responsibility, grounded in the biblical truth that children are an inheritance from the Lord and the fruit of the womb His reward.

context: the Bible says, and the children of Israel did evil again in the sight of the Lord, and the Lord delivered them into the hand of the Philistines forty years.
point_summary: Judges 13:1 provides the broad national backdrop—Israel's cycle of evil and divine judgment under the Philistines—before the narrative narrows to one particular family in that troubled context.

context: there was a certain man of Zorah of the family of the Danites whose name was Manoah, and his wife was barren and bare not
point_summary: Against the broad national backdrop of Israel's sin and Philistine oppression in verse 1, verse 2 narrows the lens to introduce one particular family in Israel — Manoah, his wife, and her barrenness — as the specific context for God's redemptive work.

context: the angel of the Lord appeared unto the woman and said unto her, Behold now, thou art barren and barest not
point_summary: The angel's announcement to a barren woman that she would conceive and bear a son establishes the divine initiative behind Samson's birth, setting the stage for God's instruction on how the child should be raised.

context: Then Manoah entreated the Lord and said, O my Lord, let the man of God which thou did send come again unto us
point_summary: Manoah's prayer in verse 8 models the posture of seeking divine instruction for child-rearing, framing the sermon's central question about how parents should order their children.

context: Manoah said, Now let thy words come to pass. How shall we order the child, and how shall we do unto him?
point_summary: Manoah's question in verse 12 — "How shall we order the child?" — frames the sermon's central concern: the God-dependent responsibility of raising children according to divine instruction.

context: the truth from Judges chapter number 13 is applicable to you and will be helpful in your life as we look at this interaction between Manoah, his wife, and the Lord.
point_summary: Manoah's prayer in verse 8 — asking God to send the angel again to teach them how to raise the promised child — frames the entire sermon as a study in parental dependence on divine instruction over self-sufficiency.

context: And so we find in our text of scripture in Judges 13 that there's a recognition of deficiency which leads to a request for direction, which leads to, as we go back to Judges 13, and really the primary focus of our message this morning, there is a robust directive. I'm thankful that God answers the prayer, the request that is given of Manoah here in Judges 13.
point_summary: Manoah's request for parenting direction in Judges 13 illustrates that recognizing one's deficiency is the necessary precondition for seeking God's wisdom, and that God responds with robust, specific direction rather than vague encouragement.

context: For lo, thou shalt conceive and bear a son, and no razor shall come on his head. For this child shall be a Nazarite unto God from the womb, and he shall begin to deliver Israel out of the hand of the Philistines.
point_summary: Manoah's prayer in verse 8 — asking how to order the child — frames the Nazarite calling announced in verse 5 as the divine directive that parents must seek God to understand and apply.

context: The man of God came unto me, and his countenance was like the countenance of an angel of God, very terrible. But I asked him not whence he was, neither told he me his name.
point_summary: Manoah's wife reports the angel's appearance and instruction to her husband, establishing that the divine directive for raising the child was first entrusted to her alone.

context: Behold, thou shalt conceive and bear a son, and now drink no wine nor strong drink, neither eat any unclean thing, for the child shall be a Nazarite to God from the womb to the day of his death.
point_summary: Manoah's wife relays the angel's announcement verbatim in verse 7, establishing the Nazarite consecration from womb to death as the foundational directive for ordering the child's life.

context: Says the angel of the Lord said unto Manoah, of all that I said unto the woman, let her beware.
point_summary: The angel's response to Manoah's question about ordering the child reiterates to him the same Nazirite dietary commands already given to the woman, framing her faithful observance as the foundational directive for the child's consecration.

context: She may not eat of anything that cometh of the vine, neither let her drink wine or strong drink, nor eat any unclean thing. All that I have commanded her, let her observe.
point_summary: The angel's repeated instruction to Manoah—that the mother must abstain from the vine, strong drink, and unclean things—is presented as the divine answer to the father's own question of how the child should be ordered.

context: In verse 15, the Bible says that Manoah said unto the angel of the Lord, I pray thee, let us detain thee until we shall have made ready a kid for thee.
point_summary: Manoah's offer of hospitality to detain the angel reveals his ignorance of the visitor's true identity, setting up the theophanic disclosure that follows.

context: The angel of the Lord said unto Manoah, though thou detain me, I will not eat of thy bread. And if thou will offer a burnt offering, thou must offer it unto the Lord. For Manoah knew not that he was an angel of the Lord.
point_summary: The angel's refusal to eat Manoah's food and his redirection — "offer it unto the Lord" — signals to the reader, before Manoah himself understands, that this visitor is no ordinary man.

context: And Manoah said unto the angel of the Lord, what is thy name? That when thy sayings come to pass, we may do thee honor.
point_summary: Manoah's question about the angel's name is met with divine mystery, the name being "secret," pointing to the transcendent, uncontainable nature of the one he had encountered.

context: And the angel of the Lord said unto him, why askest thou thus after my name, seeing it a secret?
point_summary: The angel's refusal to reveal his name — declaring it "secret" — signals the mysterious, transcendent identity of this divine messenger, pointing toward the incomprehensible nature of God himself.

context: So Manoah took a kid with a meat offering and offered it upon a rock unto the Lord. And the angel did wondrously,
point_summary: The angel's wondrous ascent in the altar flame, and Manoah's terrified recognition that he had seen God, reveal the divine identity of the messenger who had been instructing them in raising the child.

context: for it came to pass when the flame went up toward heaven from off the altar that the angel of the Lord ascended to the flame of the altar.
point_summary: The angel's ascent in the altar flame at Judges 13:20 confirms the divine identity of the messenger who delivered God's child-rearing directives to Manoah and his wife.

context: But the angel of the Lord did no more appear to Manoah and to his wife than Manoah knew that he was an angel of the Lord.
point_summary: The angel of the Lord's final disappearance in verse 21 marks the close of divine instruction, after which Manoah recognizes the messenger's identity and the full weight of God's directive for raising the child stands complete.

context: Manoah said unto his wife, we shall surely die because we have seen God.
point_summary: Manoah's fear of death upon seeing God is immediately answered by his wife's reasoned faith: divine acceptance of their offering proves God's intent was blessing, not destruction.

context: But his wife said unto him, if the Lord were pleased to kill us, he would not have received a burnt offering and a meat offering at our hands.
point_summary: Manoah's wife reasons from God's prior acts of acceptance — receiving the offering and revealing these things — that divine intent to kill them is inconsistent with such grace, modeling faith that interprets providence through God's demonstrated purposes.

context: And the woman bare a son and called his name Samson. And the child grew and the Lord blessed him.
point_summary: Samson's birth, growth, and the Lord's blessing confirm that God's detailed prenatal instructions to his parents were purposeful preparation for a child set apart to serve him.

context: And the spirit of the Lord began to move him at times in the camp of Dan between Zorah and Eshtol.
point_summary: The Spirit's movement upon the young Samson in verse 25 is read as the confirming fruit of the parents' faithful obedience to the divine directive given for his upbringing.

context: as we started the 13th chapter of the book of Judges, it was the Philistines that brought great persecution
point_summary: Israel's disobedience to God's commands in Judges 13 is cited as a historical example of what happens when generations fail to apply God's statutes, resulting in forty years of Philistine judgment.

context: receiving this announcement in Judges 13 with regard to the fact that though they had no children, that they would soon have a child
point_summary: Manoah's honest acknowledgment of his own deficiencies in Judges 13 models the posture parents need—recognizing inadequacy and bringing that need directly to God for direction in raising children.

context: This morning, we see a great example in the life of Manoah in Judges 13, an example that is applicable to every one of our lives,
point_summary: Manoah's request for divine instruction in Judges 13 serves as a model for the universal human need to acknowledge personal deficiency and seek God's direction in every area of life.

context: we come to Judges chapter number seven. And I really wanna focus on a phrase in verse 21
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The Bible says in Judges seven and verse 21, and they, that's the 300 men, we'll deal with that in just a moment, but they stood every man in his place round about the camp and all the hosts ran and cried and fled.
point_summary: The phrase "every man in his place" from Judges 7:21 is lifted as the sermon's governing theme, contrasting Israel's faithful stand here with Ezekiel's indictment that God found no man to stand in the gap.

context: the Bible says now, therefore, go to, proclaim in the ears of the people saying, whosoever is fearful and afraid, let him return and depart early from Mount Gilead. And they returned to the people 20 and 2,000 and the remain 10,000.
point_summary: God's reduction of Gideon's army from 32,000 to 10,000 by dismissing the fearful demonstrates that victory belongs to God alone, preventing Israel's pride from claiming credit.

context: the Bible tells us that both they and their camels were without number in verse number five.
point_summary: The vast, numberless host of Midian and their camels establishes the overwhelming odds against which God would reduce Gideon's army, making any victory unmistakably divine.

context: the Lord said to Gideon, the people are yet too many. Bring them down under the water and I will try them for thee there.
point_summary: Even after the fearful 22,000 departed, God declared the remaining 10,000 still too many, reserving to himself alone the sovereign decision of who would go and who would stay.

context: so he brought down the people under the water. The Lord said unto Gideon, everyone that lappeth
point_summary: God's sovereign selection of the 300 is demonstrated through the water test of Judges 7:5, where the dividing criterion was entirely God's to interpret and apply, not Gideon's.

context: of the water with his tongue as a dog lappeth, him shalt thou set by himself. Likewise, everyone that boweth down upon his knees to drink.
point_summary: God himself — not the method of drinking — made the selection at the water, using the test in verse 5 only as the means by which he divided and chose the 300 men for this specific task.

context: The Bible says the number of them that lapped, putting their hand to their mouth were 300 men. But all the rest of the people bowed down upon their knees.
point_summary: God's sovereign choice of the 300 is underscored by the fact that the selection method and which group would be chosen were determined entirely by God, not by any human quality the men displayed.

context: And the Lord said unto Gideon, By the three hundred men that laughed will I save you, and deliver the Midianites into thine hand, and let all the other people go, every man into his place.
point_summary: God's sovereign choice — not human merit or method — determined which 300 men would deliver Israel, as the selection belonged entirely to God before and after the water test.

context: Verse 8, notice the Bible says, So the people took vittles in their hand, and their trumpets, and he sent all the rest of Israel, every man into his tent, and retained those three hundred men.
point_summary: God's sovereign selection of the 300 is underscored by the dismissal of the rest — each sent to his own place — leaving only those chosen by divine decision for this specific task.

context: And notice in verse number 7, the Bible says that he gives this promise, God gives this promise by the three hundred men that laughed will I save you.
point_summary: God's sovereign selection of the 300 men who lapped—rather than the 9,700 dismissed—establishes that He assigns specific responsibility to a specific people chosen for a specific task.

context: In verse number 8, the Bible tells us that Gideon sent the other 9,700 back and those three hundred men he retained with them.
point_summary: God's reduction of Gideon's army to 300 retained men—while sending the remaining 9,700 away—establishes that those chosen bear a specific, God-assigned responsibility to accomplish what the larger group could not.

context: In verse number nine, the Bible says this, and it came to pass the same night that the Lord said unto him, this is unto Gideon, arise, get thee down unto the host, for I have delivered it into thine hand.
point_summary: God's direct command to Gideon in verse 9 — "arise, get thee down unto the host, for I have delivered it into thine hand" — illustrates the willing acceptance of assigned responsibility that the sermon identifies as embracing one's duty.

context: But look at verse number 10, if thou fear to go down, go thou with Purah thy servant down to the host, and thou shalt hear what they say, and afterward shall thine hands be strengthened to go down unto the host.
point_summary: God's provision of an optional reassurance to the fearful Gideon — "if thou fear to go down" — illustrates His gracious accommodation of human weakness as a faith-builder to strengthen obedience.

context: Understand that Gideon, when he's first approached by God, he is referred to as a mighty man of valor. In chapter six and verse number 12, you'll read that...
point_summary: The title "mighty man of valor" given to Gideon in 6:12 stands in tension with his own self-assessment as least in his father's house, illustrating that God's call precedes and exceeds the called one's confidence.

context: ...and Gideon's response in verse 15 is, I am the least in my father's house.
point_summary: Despite being called a mighty man of valor, Gideon's self-described lowliness in 6:15 frames his subsequent wavering faith and explains why God graciously provided faith-builders to strengthen him for the task.

context: if you were to read the sixth chapter, I don't have time to go back and to look at all of it, but we find that the faith builders are given, and God graciously proves to Gideon that God will indeed use Gideon to deliver his people from the Midianite oppression.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: the Midianites, and the Amalekites, and all the children of the east lay along in the valley like grasshoppers for multitude. Their camels were without number as the sand by the seaside for multitude.
context: and you pick it back up in verse number 12, and the Bible says that the Midianites, and the Amalekites, and all the children of the east lay along in the valley like grasshoppers for multitude. Their camels were without number as the sand by the seaside for multitude.
point_summary: God uses the staggering, overwhelming size of the Midianite army — innumerable as grasshoppers and sand — as the backdrop against which Gideon's final faith-building moment takes place.

reference_text: when Gideon was come, behold, there was a man that told a dream unto his fellow, and he said, Behold, I dreamed a dream, and lo, a cake of barley bread tumbled into the host of Midian, and came unto a tent, and smote it, that it fell
context: It says this in verse 13, and when Gideon was come, behold, there was a man that told a dream unto his fellow, and he said, Behold, I dreamed a dream, and lo, a cake of barley bread tumbled into the host of Midian, and came unto a tent, and smote it, that it fell
point_summary: God uses an enemy soldier's dream about a barley loaf overthrowing a tent as the final faith builder Gideon needed before leading his 300 men into battle.

context: he, speaking of Gideon, divided the 300 men into three companies. He put a trumpet in every man's hand with empty pitchers and lamps within the pitchers.
point_summary: Gideon's division of the 300 into three companies, each equipped and assigned, illustrates that embracing responsibility means being fully surrendered and yielded to a specific, communicated task.

context: And he said unto them, look on me and do likewise, and behold, when I come to the outside of the camp, it shall be that as I do, so shall ye do.
point_summary: Fulfilling responsibility requires first understanding and then embracing it, as Gideon's men demonstrated by following his direct command to watch him and do likewise.

context: When I blow with a trumpet, I and all that are with me, then blow ye the trumpets also on every side of all the camp and say, the sword of the Lord and of Gideon.
point_summary: Fulfilling one's God-given responsibility requires first understanding it and then embracing it, as Gideon's 300 men demonstrated by executing their assigned roles completely.

context: So, verse 19, Gideon and the hundred men that were with him came unto the outside of the camp in the beginning of the middle watch,
point_summary: Gideon's 300 fulfilled their assigned responsibility by executing precisely what they had been instructed — blowing trumpets, breaking pitchers, and crying the battle cry together.

context: and they stood every man in his place round about the camp, and all the hosts ran and cried and fled.
point_summary: When every man stood in his place and fulfilled his assigned responsibility, God—not the 300—received credit for the victory, which was His design from the beginning.

context: The three companies blew the trumpets and break the pitchers and held the lamps in their left hands and the trumpets in their right hands to blow with all, and they cried, the sword of the Lord and of Gideon.
point_summary: When every man stood in his place and fulfilled his assigned responsibility, God—not the 300—received credit for the victory, as the army's deliberate reduction had already made clear.

context: the task that's been assigned to us is obviously quite different than the task that we read of in Judges chapter number 7.
point_summary: Judges 7's account of a task assigned to specific men serves as a launching point for contrasting the believer's own God-given commission—carrying the gospel to a lost world—as a responsibility requiring personal accountability before God.

context: We thank you for the account in the book of Judges, having the opportunity to see that passage and that event and to understand those 300 men and their fulfillment of their responsibility that had been given to them.
point_summary: Gideon's 300 men each fulfilling their assigned post serves as the pattern for every believer faithfully occupying the place of witness God has appointed for them alone.

context: if you were to look at the book of Judges and you would understand that which takes place with Gideon
point_summary: Judges 7:2 is cited to show that God deliberately reduced Gideon's army so Israel could not claim the victory belonged to their own strength or numbers.

context: The Lord said unto Gideon, the people that are with thee are too many. They are too many for me to give the Midianites into their hands.
point_summary: God deliberately reduced Gideon's army so that Israel could claim no human credit, ensuring the only explanation for victory would be that God alone accomplished it.

context: We know the account. We know what, Judges 6 through 8, read the chapters.
point_summary: God reduces human resources to impossible odds—as with Gideon's army—so that victory cannot be credited to human wisdom or might, but glory goes to God alone.

context: Lord, if you're really going to deliver me, if you're going to use me in this manner, would you let there be some due here but not here?
point_summary: Gideon's repeated requests for confirming signs illustrate how believers can be slow to trust even clear evidence, paralleling the disciples' struggle to believe the risen Christ despite His physical presence.

context: There was another generation that knew not the Lord. What did they do? They served Balaam.
point_summary: Israel's failure in Judges 2:10—a generation rising up that knew not the Lord and turned to serve Baal—is cited as the sobering precedent for what happens when God's benefits go unremembered and unrehearsed.

context: And then you open up into the book of Judges and you find that there's another generation that rose
point_summary: Judges stands as a sobering historical witness that when God's people fail to transmit covenant truth to the next generation, an entire generation rises up that does not know the Lord.

context: remember Gideon? God comes to Gideon and says, thou mighty man of valor. The Midianites, a great host of people that were oppressing the children of Israel at that time, he says he's going to provide deliverance through Gideon. Gideon's like, you got the wrong guy. Gideon had small faith. But you know what God did for Gideon? He built his faith. You can look through those portions of Scripture and you can see in Judges 6 and 7 how that Gideon's faith is increased and God doesn't get angry with Gideon for saying, I'm going to put this fleece out and let...
point_summary: God's patient, faith-building response to Gideon's hesitancy in Judges 6–7 illustrates that God does not rebuke weak faith but graciously increases it.

context: remember Gideon? God comes to Gideon and says, thou mighty man of valor. The Midianites, a great host of people that were oppressing the children of Israel at that time, he says he's going to provide deliverance through Gideon. Gideon's like, you got the wrong guy. Gideon had small faith. But you know what God did for Gideon? He built his faith. You can look through those portions of Scripture and you can see in Judges 6 and 7 how that Gideon's faith is increased and God doesn't get angry with Gideon for saying, I'm going to put this fleece out and let...
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: How many of you know the story of Gideon, the account of Gideon? Right? Lydia, how many men did Gideon have? 300. Right? The Midianites, thousands, multitude, right? Who got the victory? Gideon. Why? God. In one word, God. Why did God tell Gideon that his army was too big? If I give you the victory with that many people, you're going to say that you got the victory.
point_summary: God's ability to deliver a great host into the hands of a small company—as with Gideon's 300—demonstrates that military outcome is determined by divine will, not numerical strength.

context: ...if you look in Judges 6 and even into chapter number 7...
point_summary: Judges 6–7 illustrates that God progressively prepares His people for assigned tasks, building faith incrementally before they are ready to fulfill what He has called them to do.

context: ...if you look in Judges 6 and even into chapter number 7...
point_summary: God's preparation of Gideon across Judges 6–7 illustrates that He develops His people's faith progressively before bringing them to the task He has appointed for them.

context: and we find that the angel of the Lord departed out of his sight in verse number 21 of Judges chapter number 6
point_summary: The angel of the Lord's departure in Judges 6:21 is identified as the first of several faith-building encounters through which God progressively prepared an initially reluctant Gideon for the task ahead.

context: and he says to God in Judges 6 in verse 36 and 37, he says this, he says, if thou wilt save Israel by mine hand as thou hast said
point_summary: Gideon's fleece request illustrates that God patiently builds a servant's faith through progressive confirmations before entrusting him with the task he was called to fulfill.

context: behold I will put a fleece of wool in the floor, and if the dew on the fleece only, be on the fleece only, and if it be dry upon all the earth beside, then shall I know that thou wilt save Israel by mine hand as thou hast said
point_summary: Gideon's fleece request in Judges 6:37 illustrates that God progressively builds a servant's faith, meeting him where he is before entrusting him with the task ahead.

context: 38 of Judges 6, and it was so. For he, Gideon, rose up early on the morrow, and thrust the fleece together, and wringed the dew out of the fleece, a bowl full of water.
point_summary: God's fulfillment of the fleece sign in Judges 6:38 is presented as a deliberate act of faith-building, preparing a still-reluctant Gideon for the task ahead.

context: In Judges 6, in verse 39, Gideon said unto God, let not thine anger be hot against me.
point_summary: Even after God answered the first fleece, Gideon's faith remained so small that he asked God a second time, reversing the sign, revealing how much further God still needed to build his servant's confidence.

context: For it was dry upon the fleece only, and there was dew on all the ground.
point_summary: God's patient accommodation of Gideon's second fleece request — reversing the sign — demonstrates that He was progressively building Gideon's faith to prepare him for the task ahead.

context: And it came to pass the same night that the Lord said unto him, arise, get thee down into the host, for I have delivered unto thine hand.
point_summary: Even after two miraculous signs with the fleece, Gideon remained fearful, so God graciously offered him a further confirmation—overhearing the enemy's dream—to strengthen his hands for battle.

context: And his fellow answered and said, this is nothing else save the sword of Gideon.
point_summary: God's patient, multi-stage preparation of Gideon—through fleeces, a night reconnaissance, and an enemy soldier's dream—demonstrates that He builds His servants' faith progressively until they are ready to act.

context: Every man doeth that which is right in his own sight, his own eyes.
point_summary: When a society abandons the authority of Scripture, moral collapse follows inevitably, as Judges 21:25 illustrates: every person becomes their own standard, doing what is right in their own eyes.

context: Every man did that was right in his own sight. Right? In his own eyes.
point_summary: When God is removed from a culture, every person becomes his own authority — the condition Judges 21:25 diagnoses as the absence of a king in Israel.

context: In the very last chapter of the book, in the very last verse, here in Judges chapter number 21 and verse number 25, in those days, there was no king in Israel. Every man did that which was right in his own eyes.
point_summary: When God is systematically removed from a culture, Judges 21:25 illustrates the inevitable result: every person becomes his own authority, mirroring the pattern of Romans 1.

context: It was a heart problem throughout the book of Judges.
point_summary: The recurring wickedness throughout Judges is cited as historical evidence that sinful human behavior is fundamentally a heart problem, not merely a social or circumstantial one.

context: In Judges chapter number two in verse number 10, the Bible tells us about this very tragic situation
point_summary: The failure of one generation to pass on the knowledge of God—illustrated by the generation in Judges 2:10 that knew neither the Lord nor His works—is presented as the tragic consequence the Joshua 4 memorials were designed to prevent.

context: In Judges chapter number two in verse number 10, the Bible says, and also all that generation were gathered under their fathers
point_summary: When a generation fails to pass on the knowledge of God, the next generation does not merely lack information — it abandons the Lord entirely and falls into idolatry.

context: And there arose another generation after them which knew not the Lord, nor yet the works which he had done for Israel
point_summary: When a generation fails to inherit the memory of God's works, the tragic result—as seen just after Joshua's time—is apostasy, underscoring why the memorial stones were divinely commanded.

context: And so what happened, the children of Israel did evil in the sight of the Lord and served Balaam
point_summary: When a generation fails to transmit knowledge of God and His works, the inevitable result is apostasy — exactly the tragedy memorials were designed to prevent.

context: They forsook the Lord God of their fathers, which brought them out of the land of Egypt and followed other gods of the people that were around about them and bowed themselves unto them and provoked the Lord to anger
point_summary: When a generation fails to remember God's works, the tragic result—as seen in Judges 2:10–12—is forsaking the Lord for other gods, illustrating why memorial practices are spiritually urgent for every succeeding generation.

context: every man did that which was right in his own sight
point_summary: Moral autonomy — every man doing what is right in his own eyes — is identified as the root impulse behind atheism: the refusal to be accountable to any authority beyond oneself.
